teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the limitation of hard-coded robot programs.
teaching_arc.progression: Introduce data registers as programmable memory, then connect them to practical uses in Fanuc code.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish seeing registers as a tool for flexibility rather than just another syntax item.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Show students how data registers make robot programs flexible, parameterized, and easier to debug.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand what data registers store and why that matters.
Students should see how registers connect to offsets, logic, and reusable programs.
Students should be able to explain when using a register is better than hard-coding a value.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Explain what changes when a value moves from code into a register.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: Goal: students can store numbers, do arithmetic, use IF/loops, capture timer, and do basic PR add/sub.

slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into By the end of today, you can… so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame DATA Registers (R) + Position Registers (PR) as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep DATA Registers (R) + Position Registers (PR) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how DATA Registers (R) + Position Registers (PR) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

Use registers for loops and IF conditions

slides.narration_seed: Keep What is a Data Register (R[n])? efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: A data register is a general-purpose storage location (a variable) for numbers
Stores integers or real (decimal) values
Used for: program logic, calculations, counting, decisions, motion-related numbers
Most controllers provide up to 200 registers: R[1] … R[200]

slides.narration_seed: Use Key points you should remember as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Storage & access: registers are global inside the controller → any program can read/write them
Programming: use them to store intermediate results (like a calculator memory)
Control flow: use in IF statements and loops (count cycles, limit retries, etc.)
Students should understand what data registers store and why that matters.
Applications: palletizing, sorting, quality checks, counters, simple decision logic

slide_ref.source_title: 3 common register types you will see

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for 3 common register types you will see using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Data Registers (R[n]) → store numbers (int/real)
Position Registers (PR[n]) → store positions (Cartesian or Joint)
Pallet Registers (PL[n]) → palletizing position data
Today: focus on R and PR (Lab 7)

slide_ref.source_title: Rules that cause the most errors

slides.narration_seed: Define Rules that cause the most errors in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Rule 1: Up to five operators on one line (long expressions can fail)
Rule 2: You can mix + with - on one line
Rule 3: You can mix * with / on one line
Rule 4: You cannot mix {+,-} with {*,/} on the same line
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Rules that cause the most errors to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Rules that cause the most errors is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: why does this fail? into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Student writes: R[2] = R[3] + R[4] * R[5] and the controller rejects it. Best explanation + fix?
FANUC register math has operator mixing restrictions. Use a temp register to split the expression.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: why does this fail? and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: why does this fail? without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: why does this fail? into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: why does this fail? question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: MOD vs DIV (useful for indexing + patterns)

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on MOD vs DIV (useful for indexing + patterns) clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: DIV = integer part of division (quotient without decimals)
MOD = remainder part (what is left after dividing)
Example (concept): 17 DIV 5 = 3, and 17 MOD 5 = 2
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Lab manual shows integer-division idea as:
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Quiz: why does this fail? into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on MOD vs DIV (useful for indexing + patterns) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of MOD vs DIV (useful for indexing + patterns) and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: pick MOD or DIV into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You want to cycle through 4 positions repeatedly: 0,1,2,3,0,1,2,3… using a counter R[1] that keeps increasing. Which gives the repeating index?
MOD gives the repeating remainder (0..3). DIV gives the growing block number (0,0,0,0,1,1,1,1...).
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: pick MOD or DIV and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: pick MOD or DIV without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: pick MOD or DIV into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: pick MOD or DIV question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: How registers are used in real programs

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for How registers are used in real programs using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: 1) Math calculations (store intermediate results)
2) Store motion-related numbers (speed/offset values)
3) Loop counters (repeat N times safely)
4) IF logic (decisions based on measurements)

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: off-by-one check into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: If you want the loop body to run exactly 10 times, which condition is correct (assuming you increment first)?
With increment-first, values go 1..10. When it becomes 10, < 10 is false, so it stops after 10 runs.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slide_ref.source_title: Quiz: why is my timer always 0?

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: why is my timer always 0? into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Student runs the program and R[10] is always 0. Most likely mistake?
If the timer never starts (or is reset after start), the value you capture will be 0 or near 0.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slides.narration_seed: Define Position Registers (PR[n]) — what they store in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: A Position Register holds a robot pose
Typical data: X, Y, Z, W, P, R (Cartesian) or joint values
Common sources you can load from:
LPOS (current Cartesian position) • JPOS (current joint position)
